plainMeaning: I bow in reverence to the first twelve Tīrthaṅkaras, naming each one from Ṛṣabha and Ajita down to Vāsupūjya.
recitation: vande usabhaṁ ajiyaṁ, saṁbhava-m-abhinandaṇa sumai paümappaha supāsaṁ; sasi pupphadaṁta sīyala, sijjhaṁsaṁ vāsupujjaṁ ca.

wordByWord.etymology: From √vand (Skt. vandate, 'to salute, praise'); first-person singular. A single 'vande' governs every name that follows.
wordByWord.notes: The lone main verb of the verse; all twelve names are its objects.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit usabha < Skt. ṛṣabha, 'bull'; accusative -ṁ as object of vande.
Skt. sambhava, 'origin, arising'; the final -ṁ is elided into the euphonic liaison with the next word.
Skt. abhinandana, 'rejoicing, gladdening'; the leading m- is a euphonic hiatus-breaker (sandhi) joining saṁbhava + abhinandaṇa.
wordByWord.notes: The prefixed m is not part of the name; it is a metrical glide typical of Prakrit gāthā.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit sumai < Skt. sumati, 'good-minded, of right intellect'.
Prakrit paüma (< Skt. padma, 'lotus') + ppaha (< prabhā, 'radiance'): 'lotus-radiant'.
wordByWord.notes: Uses the same paüma element glossed in the preface.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit sasi < Skt. śaśin, 'moon' (lit. 'hare-marked'); here a metonym for Candraprabha, 'moon-radiant'.
wordByWord.notes: The only name in the verse given by lunar epithet rather than its formal title — a deliberate poetic metonymy.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit puppha (< Skt. puṣpa, 'flower') + daṁta (< danta, 'tooth'): 'flower-toothed'.
wordByWord.notes: Also called Suvidhi; the book's spelling પુફ્ફદંત is kept verbatim.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit sijjhaṁsa < Skt. śreyāṁsa, 'most auspicious, more excellent'; accusative.
wordByWord.notes: Book spelling સિજઝંસં retained verbatim; some recensions read sejjaṁsa/seyaṁsa.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit vāsupujja < Skt. vāsupūjya, 'worthy of worship by Vasus / by the worthy'; accusative.
wordByWord.notes: The twelfth and last name of this verse; the roll-call continues in v.21.
Caps the list of twelve and links it forward into v.21, which completes the twenty-four.

literalTranslation: I bow to Ṛṣabha, Ajita, Sambhava, and Abhinandana, to Sumati, Padmaprabha, and Supārśva; to the Moon-lord (Candraprabha), Puṣpadanta, Śītala, Śreyāṁsa, and Vāsupūjya.
idiomaticTranslation: In reverence I bow: to Ṛṣabha and Ajita, to Sambhava and Abhinandana, to Sumati, lotus-bright Padmaprabha, and Supārśva; to moon-radiant Candraprabha, to Puṣpadanta, cool Śītala, auspicious Śreyāṁsa, and Vāsupūjya too.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: Having gathered all eight Saṅgha-similes in the preceding verse and bowed to the community as the 'mine of virtues,' the hymn now pivots from simile to roll-call: it begins naming the twenty-four ford-makers one by one. This verse delivers the first twelve, from Ṛṣabha to Vāsupūjya, and the closing 'ca' deliberately leaves the count open — v.21 will name the remaining twelve and complete the twenty-four. A single 'vande' at the head governs the whole chain; the names are simply heaped in the accusative, the verse becoming a continuous act of bowing rather than a sequence of separate sentences. Two details reward attention. First, 'saṁbhava-m-abhiṇaṁdaṇa' shows the characteristic Prakrit m-glide that breaks the vowel-hiatus between two names without altering either — a metrical seam, not a syllable of the name. Second, the eighth Jina is invoked not by his title but by the bare word 'sasi,' 'the Moon': a compact metonymy for Candraprabha ('moon-radiant'), letting the poet name him by his very emblem. The verse thus moves with a chant-like momentum, each name a footfall of devotion, the cool and luminous epithets (Padmaprabha 'lotus-bright,' Sasi 'moon,' Śītala 'cool') quietly extending the hymn's recurring imagery of pure, calming radiance onto the persons of the Jinas themselves.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: Naming each Tīrthaṅkara is guṇa-smaraṇa — recollection of the qualities of those who have crossed saṁsāra and built the ford for others. The bow is not petition to a deity who intervenes; the liberated Jina is a perfected ideal, and veneration purifies the reciter's own disposition (the dṛṣṭi cleansed by reverence). By rehearsing the lineage of twenty-four across vv.20–21, the verse affirms the cyclical certainty of the path: ford-makers arise in succession, and the present community stands in unbroken continuity with the very first, Ṛṣabha.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: As the reciter speaks each name, the practice is to let the name call up the Jina — recollecting that twelve perfected beings, however cool and luminous their epithets, were once embodied seekers who completed the path. The verse trains attentive, unhurried devotion: not skimming a list, but bowing twelve distinct times in a single breath of reverence.

crossReferences.text: Logassa (Caturviṁśati-stava): 'usabham-ajiaṁ ca vande… vāsu-pujjaṁ ca' — the parallel naming of the same twelve
Nandī-sūtra opening maṅgala — source of this sajjhāy

sources.title: Caturviṁśati-stava (Logassa Sūtra) — Prakrit text with translation
sources.consultedFor: Parallel Prakrit naming of the same twelve Tīrthaṅkaras (usabha…vāsupujja) and confirmation of the accusative roll-call form

sources.title: Tirthankara
sources.consultedFor: Canonical identity and order of the twenty-four Tīrthaṅkaras and the Sanskrit equivalents of the Prakrit names (Ṛṣabha…Vāsupūjya)

sources.title: Nandi Sutra (Śvetāmbara Āgama)
sources.consultedFor: Identity of the Nandī-sūtra and its opening praise from which this sajjhāy is extracted

notes: This verse names Tīrthaṅkaras 1–12; the trailing 'ca' opens into v.21, which names 13–24 to complete the twenty-four. 'Sasi' (lit. 'moon') is read as Candraprabha (8th) by lunar metonymy — standard in this litany — rather than as a separate epithet. 'saṁbhava-m-abhiṇaṁdaṇa' is parsed with a euphonic m-sandhi joining two distinct names (Sambhava + Abhinandana). Book orthography is preserved verbatim (e.g. પુફ્ફદંત, સિજઝંસં); some recensions read seyaṁsa/sejjaṁsa for the 11th and Suvihi for the 9th. IAST transliterations follow the Gujarati source readings.
